title: IV Fluid Prescribing — Resuscitation
oneLiner: Resuscitation fluids for hypovolaemic shock – correct intravascular volume deficit rapidly while avoiding fluid overload.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Obstructive shock** (tension pneumothorax, cardiac tamponade) mimicking hypovolaemia:  
  - *Single finding* – **distended neck veins** + hypotension. In tension pneumothorax: absent breath sounds + tracheal deviation. In tamponade: muffled heart sounds + pulsus paradoxus.  
  - **Action:** Do not give fluids first – needle decompression or pericardiocentesis is life-saving.
- **Cardiogenic shock** (MI, arrhythmia) giving false hypovolaemic picture:  
  - *Single finding* – **JVP is raised**, not flat. Bilateral crackles. ECG is diagnostic.  
  - **Action:** Fluids will worsen pulmonary oedema – treat underlying cause and consider inotropes.
- **Septic shock** (distributive) with relative hypovolaemia:  
  - *Single finding* – **fever or hypothermia** + high lactate without obvious fluid loss (e.g. no vomiting, diarrhoea, bleeding).  
  - **Action:** Start antibiotics and source control immediately; fluids alone insufficient.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**  
  - Hypotension (SBP <100 or drop >40 from baseline)  
  - Tachycardia (HR >100)  
  - Altered consciousness (agitation → confusion → obtundation)  
  - Oliguria (<0.5 mL/kg/hr for 2+ hours)  
  - Dizziness, thirst, cold peripheries  
- **Key exam findings:**  
  - **Dry signs:** prolonged capillary refill >2 sec, reduced skin turgor, dry mucous membranes  
  - **Low JVP** (differentiates hypovolaemia from cardiogenic/obstructive)  
  - **Narrow pulse pressure** (hypovolaemic)  
  - **Cool, clammy skin** (hypovolaemic vs warm in anaphylactic/septic)  
  - **Decreased urine output** – bladder scan if catheter not yet placed
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Hypovolaemic shock** (most likely) – overt fluid loss (vomiting, diarrhoea, blood loss, burns)  
- **Haemorrhagic shock** – blood loss + signs of peritonism, haematemesis, melaena, trauma  
- **Cardiogenic shock** – JVP raised, crackles, ECG changes, troponin rise  
- **Obstructive shock** – JVP raised, respiratory distress, absent breath sounds (tension), pulsus paradoxus (tamponade)  
- **Distributive shock (sepsis)** – fever/hypothermia, lactate >2, source of infection  
- **Anaphylactic shock** – urticaria, wheeze, angioedema, rapid onset after trigger  
- **Neurogenic shock** – history of spinal injury, bradycardia, poikilothermia  
- **Splitter:** **JVP** – low in hypovolaemia/haemorrhage; **raised** in cardiogenic/obstructive
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**  
  - Large-bore IV access x2 (16G or larger)  
  - Bedside lactate (venous or arterial)  
  - Urine output measurement (indwelling catheter if needed)  
  - Passive leg raise (PLR) – if CO/BP increases, patient is fluid-responsive  
  - ECG – rule out STEMI, arrhythmia, pericarditis  
  - FAST scan (if trauma) – intra-abdominal free fluid  
- **Bloods:**  
  - FBC, U&E, LFTs, coagulation screen, group & save / crossmatch (if haemorrhage)  
  - Arterial blood gas (lactate, base deficit, pH)  
  - Lactate – best marker of tissue hypoperfusion  
- **Imaging:**  
  - CXR – rule out cardiomegaly (cardiogenic), pneumothorax (obstructive)  
  - CT if intra-abdominal source suspected (after stabilisation)  
- **Never-miss test:**  
  - **Pregnancy test** in any woman of childbearing age – ectopic pregnancy can present as hypovolaemic shock  
  - **Lactate + base deficit** – guides severity and response; if lactate >4, high mortality risk
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - **Principle:** Give fluid only if clearly hypovolaemic AND not in cardiogenic/obstructive shock  
  - **Crystalloid:** balanced solution (Hartmann’s, Plasmalyte) or 0.9% sodium chloride  
    - 500 mL bolus over 15 minutes, reassess (NICE CG174)  
    - Repeat up to 30 mL/kg (approx. 2L) before considering vasopressors  
  - **Haemorrhagic shock:** activate local Major Haemorrhage Protocol (MHP) immediately  
    - Give blood product (e.g. O-negative if uncrossmatched) – do NOT give crystalloid in preference  
    - Target: Hb >80 g/L, platelets >50, fibrinogen >1.5 g/L  
  - **Sepsis suspicion:** start antibiotics within 1 hour (plasma lactate, blood cultures before if no delay)  
  - **Vasopressors:** if fluid-unresponsive and MAP <65 after 1L, start norepinephrine per local protocol  
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**  
  - Reassess fluid responsiveness continuously – use PLR, urine output, lactate clearance  
  - Switch to maintenance fluids once haemodynamically stable (e.g. 1 mL/kg/hr balanced crystalloid)  
  - Monitor daily weight, fluid balance chart, creatinine  
- **Long-term / outpatient:** usually not applicable – patients are admitted  
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - **Fluids:** IV balanced crystalloid at 1 mL/kg/h once shock resolved; boluses as needed  
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** start when haemodynamically stable and no active bleeding (enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily; adjust for weight/creatinine)  
  - **Diet/NBM:** NBM until cause identified (e.g. bleeding, obstruction, ileus)  
  - **Observation frequency:** NEWS2 every 15 min until stable, then hourly x4, then 4-hourly  
    - Hourly urine output documentation
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**  
  - **Crystalloid (balanced):** Hartmann’s solution or Plasmalyte 148 – 500 mL IV bolus over 15 min, reassess. Repeat as needed.  
  - **0.9% sodium chloride:** same dose – use if balanced solutions unavailable (but beware hyperchloraemic acidosis with >3L)  
- **Haemorrhagic shock:**  
  - O-negative red cells (emergency) → crossmatched units when available  
  - Fresh frozen plasma, platelets, cryoprecipitate per MHP  
- **Vasopressors (if fluid-refractory):** Norepinephrine – [check local policy for start dose] (typically 0.05–0.5 mcg/kg/min via central line)  
- **Key contraindications:**  
  - 0.9% saline in hyperchloraemic metabolic acidosis or severe AKI – prefer balanced crystalloid  
  - Hartmann’s (calcium-containing) should not be given in same line as blood products (may coagulate) – use separate line or flush with saline. Check local blood bank policy.  
- **Most dangerous dose error:**  
  - **Giving fluids too rapidly in cardiogenic shock** – causes pulmonary oedema. Always check JVP and listen to chest before giving more than 500 mL.  
  - **Failing to activate MHP in haemorrhagic shock** – crystalloid dilutes clotting factors, worsens coagulopathy. Send urgent crossmatch + group; do not delay blood.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**  
  - Pulmonary oedema (fluid overload) – especially if undiagnosed cardiogenic or renal impairment  
  - Hyperchloraemic metabolic acidosis (excessive 0.9% saline) – impairs renal blood flow  
  - Coagulopathy (dilutional) – from excessive crystalloid in haemorrhage  
  - Hypothermia (cold fluids) – warm if needed  
  - Electrolyte disturbances (e.g. hyponatraemia, hyperkalaemia) – monitor daily  
- **Chronic:** not applicable for acute resuscitation.
sections.Prognosis: - Mortality depends on aetiology: haemorrhagic shock mortality 20–40% if uncontrolled. With timely resuscitation and definitive source control, majority survive. Persistent lactate >4 or base deficit >-6 after 1 hour predicts >50% mortality.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Use balanced crystalloid as default** – evidence suggests less hyperchloraemic acidosis and renal injury than 0.9% saline.  
- **Perform passive leg raise** (PLR) to assess fluid responsiveness – if CO/BP rises, patient needs more fluid. Do not give fluid blindly.  
- **Activate MHP early** – if you suspect haemorrhagic shock, alert blood bank immediately, even before crossmatch results.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Giving fluids in hypotensive sepsis without starting antibiotics** – fluid alone will not treat infection. Blood cultures then antibiotics within 1 hour.  
- **Using crystalloid alone for major haemorrhage** – leads to dilutional coagulopathy and worse outcomes. Blood products are the resuscitation fluid of choice.  
- **Chasing BP with fluids in a non-responder** – if patient is not fluid-responsive (no increase in CO after 1L), switch to vasopressors, not more fluid. Avoid volume overload.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**  
  - Lightheadedness, syncope, or pre-syncope  
  - Decreased urine output (<300 mL in 8 hours)  
  - New chest pain, palpitations, or breathlessness  
- **Follow-up:** GP within 48–72 hours; if cause (e.g. gastroenteritis) persists, refer to GP.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / medical registrar immediately if:**  
  - NEWS2 ≥5  
  - Lactate >2 after 1 hour despite fluid resuscitation  
  - Urine output <0.5 mL/kg/hr for 2 consecutive hours  
  - MAP <65 after 1L fluid bolus  
- **Consider ICU referral if:**  
  - Need for vasopressors (norepinephrine) to maintain MAP  
  - Persisting lactate >4 or base deficit worse than -6  
  - GCS <13  
  - Respiratory failure (SpO2 <90% on 15L O2) or fluid-refractory shock  
- **Crash call if:**  
  - Unresponsive, no pulse, or agonal breathing – start CPR and call 2222.

rawMarkdown:
## IV Fluid Prescribing — Resuscitation
**One-liner:** Resuscitation fluids for hypovolaemic shock – correct intravascular volume deficit rapidly while avoiding fluid overload.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Obstructive shock** (tension pneumothorax, cardiac tamponade) mimicking hypovolaemia:  
  - *Single finding* – **distended neck veins** + hypotension. In tension pneumothorax: absent breath sounds + tracheal deviation. In tamponade: muffled heart sounds + pulsus paradoxus.  
  - **Action:** Do not give fluids first – needle decompression or pericardiocentesis is life-saving.
- **Cardiogenic shock** (MI, arrhythmia) giving false hypovolaemic picture:  
  - *Single finding* – **JVP is raised**, not flat. Bilateral crackles. ECG is diagnostic.  
  - **Action:** Fluids will worsen pulmonary oedema – treat underlying cause and consider inotropes.
- **Septic shock** (distributive) with relative hypovolaemia:  
  - *Single finding* – **fever or hypothermia** + high lactate without obvious fluid loss (e.g. no vomiting, diarrhoea, bleeding).  
  - **Action:** Start antibiotics and source control immediately; fluids alone insufficient.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**  
  - Hypotension (SBP <100 or drop >40 from baseline)  
  - Tachycardia (HR >100)  
  - Altered consciousness (agitation → confusion → obtundation)  
  - Oliguria (<0.5 mL/kg/hr for 2+ hours)  
  - Dizziness, thirst, cold peripheries  
- **Key exam findings:**  
  - **Dry signs:** prolonged capillary refill >2 sec, reduced skin turgor, dry mucous membranes  
  - **Low JVP** (differentiates hypovolaemia from cardiogenic/obstructive)  
  - **Narrow pulse pressure** (hypovolaemic)  
  - **Cool, clammy skin** (hypovolaemic vs warm in anaphylactic/septic)  
  - **Decreased urine output** – bladder scan if catheter not yet placed

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Hypovolaemic shock** (most likely) – overt fluid loss (vomiting, diarrhoea, blood loss, burns)  
- **Haemorrhagic shock** – blood loss + signs of peritonism, haematemesis, melaena, trauma  
- **Cardiogenic shock** – JVP raised, crackles, ECG changes, troponin rise  
- **Obstructive shock** – JVP raised, respiratory distress, absent breath sounds (tension), pulsus paradoxus (tamponade)  
- **Distributive shock (sepsis)** – fever/hypothermia, lactate >2, source of infection  
- **Anaphylactic shock** – urticaria, wheeze, angioedema, rapid onset after trigger  
- **Neurogenic shock** – history of spinal injury, bradycardia, poikilothermia  
- **Splitter:** **JVP** – low in hypovolaemia/haemorrhage; **raised** in cardiogenic/obstructive

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**  
  - Large-bore IV access x2 (16G or larger)  
  - Bedside lactate (venous or arterial)  
  - Urine output measurement (indwelling catheter if needed)  
  - Passive leg raise (PLR) – if CO/BP increases, patient is fluid-responsive  
  - ECG – rule out STEMI, arrhythmia, pericarditis  
  - FAST scan (if trauma) – intra-abdominal free fluid  
- **Bloods:**  
  - FBC, U&E, LFTs, coagulation screen, group & save / crossmatch (if haemorrhage)  
  - Arterial blood gas (lactate, base deficit, pH)  
  - Lactate – best marker of tissue hypoperfusion  
- **Imaging:**  
  - CXR – rule out cardiomegaly (cardiogenic), pneumothorax (obstructive)  
  - CT if intra-abdominal source suspected (after stabilisation)  
- **Never-miss test:**  
  - **Pregnancy test** in any woman of childbearing age – ectopic pregnancy can present as hypovolaemic shock  
  - **Lactate + base deficit** – guides severity and response; if lactate >4, high mortality risk

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - **Principle:** Give fluid only if clearly hypovolaemic AND not in cardiogenic/obstructive shock  
  - **Crystalloid:** balanced solution (Hartmann’s, Plasmalyte) or 0.9% sodium chloride  
    - 500 mL bolus over 15 minutes, reassess (NICE CG174)  
    - Repeat up to 30 mL/kg (approx. 2L) before considering vasopressors  
  - **Haemorrhagic shock:** activate local Major Haemorrhage Protocol (MHP) immediately  
    - Give blood product (e.g. O-negative if uncrossmatched) – do NOT give crystalloid in preference  
    - Target: Hb >80 g/L, platelets >50, fibrinogen >1.5 g/L  
  - **Sepsis suspicion:** start antibiotics within 1 hour (plasma lactate, blood cultures before if no delay)  
  - **Vasopressors:** if fluid-unresponsive and MAP <65 after 1L, start norepinephrine per local protocol  
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**  
  - Reassess fluid responsiveness continuously – use PLR, urine output, lactate clearance  
  - Switch to maintenance fluids once haemodynamically stable (e.g. 1 mL/kg/hr balanced crystalloid)  
  - Monitor daily weight, fluid balance chart, creatinine  
- **Long-term / outpatient:** usually not applicable – patients are admitted  
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - **Fluids:** IV balanced crystalloid at 1 mL/kg/h once shock resolved; boluses as needed  
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** start when haemodynamically stable and no active bleeding (enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily; adjust for weight/creatinine)  
  - **Diet/NBM:** NBM until cause identified (e.g. bleeding, obstruction, ileus)  
  - **Observation frequency:** NEWS2 every 15 min until stable, then hourly x4, then 4-hourly  
    - Hourly urine output documentation

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**  
  - **Crystalloid (balanced):** Hartmann’s solution or Plasmalyte 148 – 500 mL IV bolus over 15 min, reassess. Repeat as needed.  
  - **0.9% sodium chloride:** same dose – use if balanced solutions unavailable (but beware hyperchloraemic acidosis with >3L)  
- **Haemorrhagic shock:**  
  - O-negative red cells (emergency) → crossmatched units when available  
  - Fresh frozen plasma, platelets, cryoprecipitate per MHP  
- **Vasopressors (if fluid-refractory):** Norepinephrine – [check local policy for start dose] (typically 0.05–0.5 mcg/kg/min via central line)  
- **Key contraindications:**  
  - 0.9% saline in hyperchloraemic metabolic acidosis or severe AKI – prefer balanced crystalloid  
  - Hartmann’s (calcium-containing) should not be given in same line as blood products (may coagulate) – use separate line or flush with saline. Check local blood bank policy.  
- **Most dangerous dose error:**  
  - **Giving fluids too rapidly in cardiogenic shock** – causes pulmonary oedema. Always check JVP and listen to chest before giving more than 500 mL.  
  - **Failing to activate MHP in haemorrhagic shock** – crystalloid dilutes clotting factors, worsens coagulopathy. Send urgent crossmatch + group; do not delay blood.

### Complications
- **Acute:**  
  - Pulmonary oedema (fluid overload) – especially if undiagnosed cardiogenic or renal impairment  
  - Hyperchloraemic metabolic acidosis (excessive 0.9% saline) – impairs renal blood flow  
  - Coagulopathy (dilutional) – from excessive crystalloid in haemorrhage  
  - Hypothermia (cold fluids) – warm if needed  
  - Electrolyte disturbances (e.g. hyponatraemia, hyperkalaemia) – monitor daily  
- **Chronic:** not applicable for acute resuscitation.

### Prognosis
- Mortality depends on aetiology: haemorrhagic shock mortality 20–40% if uncontrolled. With timely resuscitation and definitive source control, majority survive. Persistent lactate >4 or base deficit >-6 after 1 hour predicts >50% mortality.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Use balanced crystalloid as default** – evidence suggests less hyperchloraemic acidosis and renal injury than 0.9% saline.  
- **Perform passive leg raise** (PLR) to assess fluid responsiveness – if CO/BP rises, patient needs more fluid. Do not give fluid blindly.  
- **Activate MHP early** – if you suspect haemorrhagic shock, alert blood bank immediately, even before crossmatch results.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Giving fluids in hypotensive sepsis without starting antibiotics** – fluid alone will not treat infection. Blood cultures then antibiotics within 1 hour.  
- **Using crystalloid alone for major haemorrhage** – leads to dilutional coagulopathy and worse outcomes. Blood products are the resuscitation fluid of choice.  
- **Chasing BP with fluids in a non-responder** – if patient is not fluid-responsive (no increase in CO after 1L), switch to vasopressors, not more fluid. Avoid volume overload.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**  
  - Lightheadedness, syncope, or pre-syncope  
  - Decreased urine output (<300 mL in 8 hours)  
  - New chest pain, palpitations, or breathlessness  
- **Follow-up:** GP within 48–72 hours; if cause (e.g. gastroenteritis) persists, refer to GP.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / medical registrar immediately if:**  
  - NEWS2 ≥5  
  - Lactate >2 after 1 hour despite fluid resuscitation  
  - Urine output <0.5 mL/kg/hr for 2 consecutive hours  
  - MAP <65 after 1L fluid bolus  
- **Consider ICU referral if:**  
  - Need for vasopressors (norepinephrine) to maintain MAP  
  - Persisting lactate >4 or base deficit worse than -6  
  - GCS <13  
  - Respiratory failure (SpO2 <90% on 15L O2) or fluid-refractory shock  
- **Crash call if:**  
  - Unresponsive, no pulse, or agonal breathing – start CPR and call 2222.
